data.reasoningText:
The project is already scaffolded with:
- .NET 10 targeting
- Microsoft.Agents.AI (for agent framework)
- Microsoft.Extensions.AI.OpenAI (for AI integration)
- OpenAI package

I need to build a research agent that:
1. Has a web search tool
2. Has a note-taking tool
3. Uses an AI agent to research a topic and summarize findings

Let me build this using the Microsoft.Extensions.AI abstractions along with Microsoft.Agents.AI. I'll create:
- A web search tool (simulated via Bing or a mock)


- A note-taking tool for storing research findings
- An agent loop that orchestrates the research workflow

For the architecture, I'll define two tools with `[Description]` attributes and `AIFunction` - one for web searching and another for managing notes - then wire them into an `IChatClient` that can invoke them iteratively to research a topic and compile findings.

Now I need to check what .NET SDK version is available and how `Microsoft.Extensions.AI` handles tool calling and the agent loop pattern.

arguments.name: explore-packages
arguments.prompt:
I need to understand the API surface available from these NuGet packages in a .NET 10 project:
- Microsoft.Agents.AI (version 1.*-*)
- Microsoft.Extensions.AI.OpenAI (version 9.*)
- OpenAI (version 2.*)

The project is at /tmp/sv-a82fd3f4ea01451ab124a263c32341e9/ResearchAgent

Please investigate:
1. What namespaces and key types are available from Microsoft.Agents.AI? Look in the NuGet cache (~/.nuget/packages/microsoft.agents.ai/) for the DLL and list its public types using `dotnet script` or by examining the package contents.
2. What types are available from Microsoft.Extensions.AI and Microsoft.Extensions.AI.OpenAI? Look at the actual resolved package versions.
3. Specifically, I want to know:
   - How to create an IChatClient from OpenAI
   - How to define tools/functions (AIFunction, AITool, etc.)
   - How to run an agent loop with automatic tool calling
   - What is the ChatClientBuilder API
   - Is there a FunctionInvokingChatClient or similar middleware?

Look in the NuGet cache at ~/.nuget/packages/ for the actual DLLs and examine their XML doc files or use reflection. Also check if there are any README files in the package folders.

Run: find ~/.nuget/packages/microsoft.extensions.ai -name "*.xml" -o -name "README*" | head -20
And: find ~/.nuget/packages/microsoft.agents.ai -name "*.xml" -o -name "README*" | head -20
And examine the XML docs to understand the APIs.

arguments.command:
# Look at key API members
cd ~/.nuget/packages/microsoft.extensions.ai/10.4.1/lib/net10.0/
grep -A3 'name="M:Microsoft.Extensions.AI.FunctionInvokingChatClient.#ctor' Microsoft.Extensions.AI.xml | head -20
echo "=== ChatClientBuilder ==="
grep 'name="M:Microsoft.Extensions.AI.ChatClientBuilder' Microsoft.Extensions.AI.xml | head -20
echo "=== FunctionInvokingChatClientBuilderExtensions ==="
grep -A5 'name="M:Microsoft.Extensions.AI.FunctionInvokingChatClientBuilderExtensions' Microsoft.Extensions.AI.xml | head -20

# Look at OpenAIClientExtensions for creating IChatClient
grep -A10 'name="M:Microsoft.Extensions.AI.OpenAIClientExtensions' ~/.nuget/packages/microsoft.extensions.ai.openai/10.4.1/lib/net10.0/Microsoft.Extensions.AI.OpenAI.xml | head -40

# Check M.E.AI.Abstractions for AIFunction, AITool
find ~/.nuget/packages/microsoft.extensions.ai.abstractions/ -name "*.xml" -path "*net10*" | head -3 && echo "---" && grep 'name="T:' ~/.nuget/packages/microsoft.extensions.ai.abstractions/10.4.1/lib/net10.0/Microsoft.Extensions.AI.Abstractions.xml 2>/dev/null | sed 's/.*name="T://' | sed 's/".*//' | sort | head -60

data.reasoningText:
Now I have a good understanding of the API surface. Let me look at key APIs more closely:

1. `FunctionInvokingChatClient` - middleware that auto-invokes tools
2. `ChatClientBuilder` - for building chat client pipelines
3. `OpenAIChatClient` / `OpenAIClientExtensions` - for creating the client
4. `Microsoft.Agents.AI.ChatClientAgent` - agent framework

Let me look at the specific APIs I need.

        <member name="M:Microsoft.Extensions.AI.OpenAIClientExtensions.AsIChatClient(OpenAI.Assistants.AssistantClient,System.String,System.String)">
            <summary>Gets an <see cref="T:Microsoft.Extensions.AI.IChatClient"/> for use with this <see cref="T:OpenAI.Assistants.AssistantClient"/>.</summary>
            <param name="assistantClient">The <see cref="T:OpenAI.Assistants.AssistantClient"/> instance to be accessed as an <see cref="T:Microsoft.Extensions.AI.IChatClient"/>.</param>
            <param name="assistantId">The unique identifier of the assistant with which to interact.</param>
            <param name="threadId">
            An optional existing thread identifier for the chat session. This serves as a default, and may be overridden per call to
            <see cref="M:Microsoft.Extensions.AI.IChatClient.GetResponseAsync(System.Collections.Generic.IEnumerable{Microsoft.Extensions.AI.ChatMessage},Microsoft.Extensions.AI.ChatOptions,System.Threading.CancellationToken)"/> or <see cref="M:Microsoft.Extensions.AI.IChatClient.GetStreamingResponseAsync(System.Collections.Generic.IEnumerable{Microsoft.Extensions.AI.ChatMessage},Microsoft.Extensions.AI.ChatOptions,System.Threading.CancellationToken)"/> via the <see cref="P:Microsoft.Extensions.AI.ChatOptions.ConversationId"/>
            property. If no thread ID is provided via either mechanism, a new thread will be created for the request.
            </param>
            <returns>An <see cref="T:Microsoft.Extensions.AI.IChatClient"/> instance configured to interact with the specified agent and thread.</returns>
            <exception cref="T:System.ArgumentNullException"><paramref name="assistantClient"/> is <see langword="null"/>.</exception>

1. # About Microsoft Agent Framework
2. 
3. Microsoft Agent Framework is a comprehensive .NET library for building, orchestrating, and deploying AI agents and multi-agent workflows. The framework provides everything from simple chat agents to complex multi-agent systems with graph-based orchestration capabilities.
4. 
5. ## Key Features
6. 
7. - **Multi-Agent Orchestration**: Coordinate multiple agents using sequential, concurrent, group chat, and handoff patterns
8. - **Graph-based Workflows**: Connect agents and functions with streaming, checkpointing, and human-in-the-loop capabilities, with both imperative or declarative workflow support
9. - **Multiple Provider Support**: Seamlessly integrate with various LLM providers with more being added continuously
10. - **Extensible Middleware**: Flexible request/response processing with custom pipelines and exception handling
11. - **Built-in Observability**: OpenTelemetry integration for distributed tracing, monitoring, and debugging
12. - **Cross-Platform**: Compatible with .NET 8.0, .NET Standard 2.0, and .NET Framework for broad deployment options
13. 
14. Whether you're building simple AI assistants or complex multi-agent systems, Microsoft Agent Framework provides the tools and abstractions needed to create robust, scalable AI applications in .NET.
15. 
16. # Getting Started ⚡
17. 
18. - Learn more at the [documentation site](https://learn.microsoft.com/agent-framework/overview/agent-framework-overview).
19. - Join the [Discord community](https://discord.gg/b5zjErwbQM).
20. - Follow the team on [Semantic Kernel blog](https://devblogs.microsoft.com/semantic-kernel/).
21. - Check out the [GitHub repository](https://github.com/microsoft/agent-framework) for the latest updates.
22.

 # About Microsoft Agent Framework
 
 Microsoft Agent Framework is a comprehensive .NET library for building, orchestrating, and deploying AI agents and multi-agent workflows. The framework provides everything from simple chat agents to complex multi-agent systems with graph-based orchestration capabilities.
 
 ## Key Features
 
 - **Multi-Agent Orchestration**: Coordinate multiple agents using sequential, concurrent, group chat, and handoff patterns
 - **Graph-based Workflows**: Connect agents and functions with streaming, checkpointing, and human-in-the-loop capabilities, with both imperative or declarative workflow support
 - **Multiple Provider Support**: Seamlessly integrate with various LLM providers with more being added continuously
 - **Extensible Middleware**: Flexible request/response processing with custom pipelines and exception handling
 - **Built-in Observability**: OpenTelemetry integration for distributed tracing, monitoring, and debugging
 - **Cross-Platform**: Compatible with .NET 8.0, .NET Standard 2.0, and .NET Framework for broad deployment options
 
 Whether you're building simple AI assistants or complex multi-agent systems, Microsoft Agent Framework provides the tools and abstractions needed to create robust, scalable AI applications in .NET.
 
 # Getting Started ⚡
 
 - Learn more at the [documentation site](https://learn.microsoft.com/agent-framework/overview/agent-framework-overview).
 - Join the [Discord community](https://discord.gg/b5zjErwbQM).
 - Follow the team on [Semantic Kernel blog](https://devblogs.microsoft.com/semantic-kernel/).
 - Check out the [GitHub repository](https://github.com/microsoft/agent-framework) for the latest updates.

1. # Microsoft.Extensions.AI
2. 
3. .NET developers need to integrate and interact with a growing variety of artificial intelligence (AI) services in their apps. The `Microsoft.Extensions.AI` libraries provide a unified approach for representing generative AI components, and enable seamless integration and interoperability with various AI services.
4. 
5. ## The packages
6. 
7. The [Microsoft.Extensions.AI.Abstractions](https://www.nuget.org/packages/Microsoft.Extensions.AI.Abstractions) package provides the core exchange types, including [`IChatClient`](https://learn.microsoft.com/dotnet/api/microsoft.extensions.ai.ichatclient) and [`IEmbeddingGenerator<TInput,TEmbedding>`](https://learn.microsoft.com/dotnet/api/microsoft.extensions.ai.iembeddinggenerator-2). Any .NET library that provides an LLM client can implement the `IChatClient` interface to enable seamless integration with consuming code.
8. 
9. The [Microsoft.Extensions.AI](https://www.nuget.org/packages/Microsoft.Extensions.AI) package has an implicit dependency on the `Microsoft.Extensions.AI.Abstractions` package. This package enables you to easily integrate components such as automatic function tool invocation, telemetry, and caching into your applications using familiar dependency injection and middleware patterns. For example, it provides the [`UseOpenTelemetry(ChatClientBuilder, ILoggerFactory, String, Action<OpenTelemetryChatClient>)`](https://learn.microsoft.com/dotnet/api/microsoft.extensions.ai.opentelemetrychatclientbuilderextensions.useopentelemetry#microsoft-extensions-ai-opentelemetrychatclientbuilderextensions-useopentelemetry(microsoft-extensions-ai-chatclientbuilder-microsoft-extensions-logging-iloggerfactory-system-string-system-action((microsoft-extensions-ai-opentelemetrychatclient)))) extension method, which adds OpenTelemetry support to the chat client pipeline.
10. 
11. ## Which package to reference
12. 
13. Libraries that provide implementations of the abstractions typically reference only `Microsoft.Extensions.AI.Abstractions`.
14. 
15. To also have access to higher-level utilities for working with generative AI components, reference the `Microsoft.Extensions.AI` package instead (which itself references `Microsoft.Extensions.AI.Abstractions`). Most consuming applications and services should reference the `Microsoft.Extensions.AI` package along with one or more libraries that provide concrete implementations of the abstractions.
16. 
17. ## Install the package
18. 
19. From the command-line:
20. 
21. ```console
22. dotnet add package Microsoft.Extensions.AI
23. ```
24. 
25. Or directly in the C# project file:
26. 
27. ```xml
28. <ItemGroup>
29.   <PackageReference Include="Microsoft.Extensions.AI" Version="[CURRENTVERSION]" />
30. </ItemGroup>
31. ```
32. 
33. ## Documentation
34. 
35. Refer to the [Microsoft.Extensions.AI libraries documentation](https://learn.microsoft.com/dotnet/ai/microsoft-extensions-ai) for more information and API usage examples.
36. 
37. ## Feedback & Contributing
38. 
39. We welcome feedback and contributions in [our GitHub repo](https://github.com/dotnet/extensions).
40.

@@ -1,40 +1,40 @@
 # Microsoft.Extensions.AI
 
 .NET developers need to integrate and interact with a growing variety of artificial intelligence (AI) services in their apps. The `Microsoft.Extensions.AI` libraries provide a unified approach for representing generative AI components, and enable seamless integration and interoperability with various AI services.
 
 ## The packages
 
 The [Microsoft.Extensions.AI.Abstractions](https://www.nuget.org/packages/Microsoft.Extensions.AI.Abstractions) package provides the core exchange types, including [`IChatClient`](https://learn.microsoft.com/dotnet/api/microsoft.extensions.ai.ichatclient) and [`IEmbeddingGenerator<TInput,TEmbedding>`](https://learn.microsoft.com/dotnet/api/microsoft.extensions.ai.iembeddinggenerator-2). Any .NET library that provides an LLM client can implement the `IChatClient` interface to enable seamless integration with consuming code.
 
 The [Microsoft.Extensions.AI](https://www.nuget.org/packages/Microsoft.Extensions.AI) package has an implicit dependency on the `Microsoft.Extensions.AI.Abstractions` package. This package enables you to easily integrate components such as automatic function tool invocation, telemetry, and caching into your applications using familiar dependency injection and middleware patterns. For example, it provides the [`UseOpenTelemetry(ChatClientBuilder, ILoggerFactory, String, Action<OpenTelemetryChatClient>)`](https://learn.microsoft.com/dotnet/api/microsoft.extensions.ai.opentelemetrychatclientbuilderextensions.useopentelemetry#microsoft-extensions-ai-opentelemetrychatclientbuilderextensions-useopentelemetry(microsoft-extensions-ai-chatclientbuilder-microsoft-extensions-logging-iloggerfactory-system-string-system-action((microsoft-extensions-ai-opentelemetrychatclient)))) extension method, which adds OpenTelemetry support to the chat client pipeline.
 
 ## Which package to reference
 
 Libraries that provide implementations of the abstractions typically reference only `Microsoft.Extensions.AI.Abstractions`.
 
 To also have access to higher-level utilities for working with generative AI components, reference the `Microsoft.Extensions.AI` package instead (which itself references `Microsoft.Extensions.AI.Abstractions`). Most consuming applications and services should reference the `Microsoft.Extensions.AI` package along with one or more libraries that provide concrete implementations of the abstractions.
 
 ## Install the package
 
 From the command-line:
 
 ```console
 dotnet add package Microsoft.Extensions.AI
 ```
 
 Or directly in the C# project file:
 
 ```xml
 <ItemGroup>
   <PackageReference Include="Microsoft.Extensions.AI" Version="[CURRENTVERSION]" />
 </ItemGroup>
 ```
 
 ## Documentation
 
 Refer to the [Microsoft.Extensions.AI libraries documentation](https://learn.microsoft.com/dotnet/ai/microsoft-extensions-ai) for more information and API usage examples.
 
 ## Feedback & Contributing
 
 We welcome feedback and contributions in [our GitHub repo](https://github.com/dotnet/extensions).

810.         <member name="P:OpenAI.Assistants.AssistantCreationOptions.NucleusSamplingFactor">
811.              <summary>
812.              An alternative to sampling with temperature, called nucleus sampling, where the model considers the results of the tokens with top_p probability mass. So 0.1 means only the tokens comprising the top 10% probability mass are considered.
813.             
814.              We generally recommend altering this or temperature but not both.
815.              </summary>
816.         </member>

844.         <member name="P:OpenAI.Assistants.AssistantModificationOptions.NucleusSamplingFactor">
845.              <summary>
846.              An alternative to sampling with temperature, called nucleus sampling, where the model considers the results of the tokens with top_p probability mass. So 0.1 means only the tokens comprising the top 10% probability mass are considered.
847.             
848.              We generally recommend altering this or temperature but not both.
849.              </summary>
850.         </member>

857.             The resources are specific to the type of tool.
858.             For example, the `code_interpreter` tool requires a list of file IDs, while the `file_search` tool requires a list of vector store IDs.

1506.             </remarks>

1508.         <member name="P:OpenAI.Assistants.RunCreationOptions.ToolsOverride">
1509.             <summary>
1510.             A run-specific collection of tool definitions that will override the assistant-level defaults. If not provided,
1511.             the assistant's defined tools will be used. Available tools include:
1512.             <para>
1513.             <list type="bullet">
1514.             <item>
1515.                 <c>code_interpreter</c> - <see cref="T:OpenAI.Assistants.CodeInterpreterToolDefinition"/> 
1516.                 - works with data, math, and computer code
1517.             </item>
1518.             <item>
1519.                 <c>file_search</c> - <see cref="T:OpenAI.Assistants.FileSearchToolDefinition"/> 
1520.                 - dynamically enriches an Run's context with content from vector stores
1521.             </item>
1522.             <item>
1523.                 <c>function</c> - <see cref="T:OpenAI.Assistants.FunctionToolDefinition"/>
1524.                 - enables caller-provided custom functions for actions and enrichment
1525.             </item>
1526.             </list>
1527.             </para>
1528.             </summary>
1529.         </member>
1530.         <member name="P:OpenAI.Assistants.RunCreationOptions.Metadata">
1531.             <summary> Set of 16 key-value pairs that can be attached to an object. This can be useful for storing additional information about the object in a structured format. Keys can be a maximum of 64 characters long and values can be a maximum of 512 characters long. </summary>
1532.         </member>
1533.         <member name="P:OpenAI.Assistants.RunCreationOptions.Temperature">
1534.             <summary> What sampling temperature to use, between 0 and 2. Higher values like 0.8 will make the output more random, while lower values like 0.2 will make it more focused and deterministic. </summary>
1535.         </member>
1536.         <member name="P:OpenAI.Assistants.RunCreationOptions.NucleusSamplingFactor">
1537.              <summary>
1538.              An alternative to sampling with temperature, called nucleus sampling, where the model considers the results of the tokens with top_p probability mass. So 0.1 means only the tokens comprising the top 10% probability mass are considered.
1539.             
1540.              We generally recommend altering this or temperature but not both.
1541.              </summary>
1542.         </member>
1543.         <member name="P:OpenAI.Assistants.RunCreationOptions.MaxInputTokenCount">
1544.             <summary> The maximum number of prompt tokens that may be used over the course of the run. The run will make a best effort to use only the number of prompt tokens specified, across multiple turns of the run. If the run exceeds the number of prompt tokens specified, the run will end with status `incomplete`. See `incomplete_details` for more info. </summary>
1545.         </member>
1546.         <member name="P:OpenAI.Assistants.RunCreationOptions.MaxOutputTokenCount">
1547.             <summary> The maximum number of completion tokens that may be used over the course of the run. The run will make a best effort to use only the number of completion tokens specified, across multiple turns of the run. If the run exceeds the number of completion tokens specified, the run will end with status `incomplete`. See `incomplete_details` for more info. </summary>
1548.         </member>

1744.             <summary>
1745.             Represents a single item of streamed Assistants API data.
1746.             </summary>
1747.             <remarks>
1748.             Please note that this is the abstract base type. To access data, downcast an instance of this type to an
1749.             appropriate, derived update type:
1750.             <para>
1751.             For messages: <see cref="T:OpenAI.Assistants.MessageStatusUpdate"/>, <see cref="T:OpenAI.Assistants.MessageContentUpdate"/>
1752.             </para>
1753.             <para>
1754.             For runs and run steps: <see cref="T:OpenAI.Assistants.RunUpdate"/>, <see cref="T:OpenAI.Assistants.RunStepUpdate"/>, <see cref="T:OpenAI.Assistants.RunStepDetailsUpdate"/>,
1755.             <see cref="T:OpenAI.Assistants.RequiredActionUpdate"/>
1756.             </para>
1757.             <para>
1758.             For threads: <see cref="T:OpenAI.Assistants.ThreadUpdate"/>
1759.             </para>
1760.             </remarks>
1761.         </member>
1762.         <member name="P:OpenAI.Assistants.StreamingUpdate.UpdateKind">
1763.             <summary>
1764.             A value indicating what type of event this update represents.
1765.             </summary>
1766.             <remarks>
1767.             Many events share the same response type. For example, <see cref="F:OpenAI.Assistants.StreamingUpdateReason.RunCreated"/> and
1768.             <see cref="F:OpenAI.Assistants.StreamingUpdateReason.RunCompleted"/> are both associated with a <see cref="T:OpenAI.Assistants.ThreadRun"/> instance.
1769.             You can use the value of <see cref="P:OpenAI.Assistants.StreamingUpdate.UpdateKind"/> to differentiate between these events when the type is not
1770.             sufficient to do so.
1771.             </remarks>
1772.         </member>

2872.         <member name="T:OpenAI.Chat.ChatCompletion">
2873.             <summary> A chat completion generated by the model. </summary>
2874.         </member>
2875.         <member name="P:OpenAI.Chat.ChatCompletion.Object">
2876.             <summary> The object type, which is always `chat.completion`. </summary>
2877.         </member>
2878.         <member name="P:OpenAI.Chat.ChatCompletion.CreatedAt">
2879.             <summary> The timestamp when the chat completion was created. </summary>
2880.         </member>
2881.         <member name="P:OpenAI.Chat.ChatCompletion.FinishReason">
2882.             <summary>
2883.                 The reason the model stopped generating tokens. This will be:
2884.                 <list>
2885.                     <item>
2886.                         <see cref="F:OpenAI.Chat.ChatFinishReason.Stop"/> if the model hit a natural stop point or a provided stop sequence
2887.                     </item>
2888.                     <item>
2889.                         <see cref="F:OpenAI.Chat.ChatFinishReason.Length"/> if the maximum number of tokens specified in the request was reached
2890.                     </item>
2891.                     <item>
2892.                         <see cref="F:OpenAI.Chat.ChatFinishReason.ContentFilter"/> if content was omitted due to a flag from our content filters
2893.                     </item>
2894.                     <item>
2895.                         <see cref="F:OpenAI.Chat.ChatFinishReason.ToolCalls"/> if the model called a tool
2896.                     </item>
2897.                     <item>
2898.                         <see cref="F:OpenAI.Chat.ChatFinishReason.FunctionCall"/> (obsolte) if the model called a function
2899.                     </item>
2900.                 </list>
2901.             </summary>
2902.         </member>
2903.         <member name="P:OpenAI.Chat.ChatCompletion.ContentTokenLogProbabilities">
2904.             <summary> The message content tokens with log probability information. </summary>
2905.         </member>
2906.         <member name="P:OpenAI.Chat.ChatCompletion.RefusalTokenLogProbabilities">
2907.             <summary> The message refusal tokens with log probability information. </summary>
2908.         </member>
2909.         <member name="P:OpenAI.Chat.ChatCompletion.Role">
2910.             <summary> The role of the author of this message. </summary>
2911.         </member>
2912.         <member name="P:OpenAI.Chat.ChatCompletion.Content">
2913.             <summary> The contents of the message. </summary>
2914.         </member>
2915.         <member name="P:OpenAI.Chat.ChatCompletion.ToolCalls">
2916.             <summary> The tool calls generated by the model, such as function calls. </summary>
2917.         </member>
2918.         <member name="P:OpenAI.Chat.ChatCompletion.Refusal">
2919.             <summary> The refusal message generated by the model. </summary>
2920.         </member>
2921.         <member name="P:OpenAI.Chat.ChatCompletion.OutputAudio">
2922.             <summary> The audio response generated by the model. </summary>
2923.         </member>
2924.         <member name="T:OpenAI.Chat.ChatCompletionOptions">
2925.             <summary>
2926.             Request-level options for chat completion.
2927.             </summary>
2928.         </member>
2929.         <member name="P:OpenAI.Chat.ChatCompletionOptions.Messages">
2930.             <summary>
2931.             A list of messages comprising the conversation so far. [Example Python code](https://cookbook.openai.com/examples/how_to_format_inputs_to_chatgpt_models).
2932.             Please note <see cref="T:OpenAI.Chat.ChatMessage"/> is the base class. According to the scenario, a derived class of the base class might need to be assigned here, or this property needs to be casted to one of the possible derived classes.
2933.             The available derived classes include <see cref="T:OpenAI.Chat.AssistantChatMessage"/>, <see cref="T:OpenAI.Chat.FunctionChatMessage"/>, <see cref="T:OpenAI.Chat.SystemChatMessage"/>, <see cref="T:OpenAI.Chat.ToolChatMessage"/> and <see cref="T:OpenAI.Chat.UserChatMessage"/>.
2934.             </summary>
2935.         </member>
2936.         <member name="P:OpenAI.Chat.ChatCompletionOptions.Model">
2937.             <summary>
2938.             ID of the model to use. See the <see href="https://platform.openai.com/docs/models/model-endpoint-compatibility">model endpoint compatibility</see> table for details on which models work with the Chat API.
2939.             </summary>
2940.         </member>
2941.         <member name="P:OpenAI.Chat.ChatCompletionOptions.N">
2942.             <summary> How many chat completion choices to generate for each input message. Note that you will be charged based on the number of generated tokens across all of the choices. Keep `n` as `1` to minimize costs. </summary>
2943.         </member>
2944.         <member name="P:OpenAI.Chat.ChatCompletionOptions.Stream">
2945.             <summary> If set, partial message deltas will be sent, like in ChatGPT. Tokens will be sent as data-only [server-sent events](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/Server-sent_events/Using_server-sent_events#Event_stream_format) as they become available, with the stream terminated by a `data: [DONE]` message. [Example Python code](https://cookbook.openai.com/examples/how_to_stream_completions). </summary>
2946.         </member>
2947.         <member name="P:OpenAI.Chat.ChatCompletionOptions.StreamOptions">
2948.             <summary> Gets or sets the stream options. </summary>
2949.         </member>
2950.         <member name="P:OpenAI.Chat.ChatCompletionOptions.IncludeLogProbabilities">
2951.             <summary> Whether to return log probabilities of the output tokens or not. If true, returns the log probabilities of each output token returned in the message content. </summary>
2952.         </member>
2953.         <member name="P:OpenAI.Chat.ChatCompletionOptions.TopLogProbabilityCount">
2954.             <summary> An integer between 0 and 20 specifying the number of most likely tokens to return at each token position, each with an associated log probability. <see cref="P:OpenAI.Chat.ChatCompletionOptions.IncludeLogProbabilities"/> must be set to <see langword="true"/> if this property is used. </summary>
2955.         </member>
2956.         <member name="P:OpenAI.Chat.ChatCompletionOptions.StopSequences">
2957.             <summary> Up to 4 sequences where the API will stop generating further tokens. </summary>
2958.         </member>
2959.         <member name="P:OpenAI.Chat.ChatCompletionOptions.LogitBiases">
2960.             <summary>
2961.             Modifies the likelihood of specified tokens appearing in the completion. It maps tokens (specified by their token ID in the tokenizer) to an associated bias value from -100 to 100. Mathematically, the bias is added to the logits generated by the model prior to sampling. The exact effect will vary per model, but values between -1 and 1 should decrease or increase likelihood of selection; values like -100 or 100 should result in a ban or exclusive selection of the relevant token.
2962.             </summary>
2963.         </member>
2964.         <member name="P:OpenAI.Chat.ChatCompletionOptions.ToolChoice">
2965.             <summary>
2966.             Specifies which tool is called by the model, if any. <see cref="!:ChatToolChoice.None"/> means the model will not call any tool and instead generates a message. <see cref="!:ChatToolChoice.Auto"/> means the model can pick between generating a message or calling one or more tools.
2967.             <see cref="!:ChatToolChoice.Required"/> means the model must call one or more tools. The model can also be forced to call a specific tool by constructing a new instance of <see cref="T:OpenAI.Chat.ChatToolChoice"/> while passing the desired <see cref="T:OpenAI.Chat.ChatTool"/> as a constructor parameter.
2968.             <remarks>
2969.             <see cref="!:ChatToolChoice.None"/> is the default behavior when no tools are present, while <see cref="!:ChatToolChoice.Auto"/> is the default if tools are present.
2970.             </remarks>
2971.             </summary>
2972.         </member>
2973.         <member name="P:OpenAI.Chat.ChatCompletionOptions.AllowParallelToolCalls">
2974.             <summary>
2975.             Whether to enable parallel function calling during tool use.
2976.             </summary>
2977.             <remarks>
2978.             Assumed <c>true</c> if not otherwise specified.
2979.             </remarks>
2980.         </member>
2981.         <member name="P:OpenAI.Chat.ChatCompletionOptions.EndUserId">
2982.             <summary>
2983.                 A unique identifier representing your end-user, which can help OpenAI to monitor and detect abuse.
2984.                 <see href="https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/safety-best-practices/end-user-ids">Learn more</see>.
2985.             </summary>
2986.         </member>
2987.         <member name="P:OpenAI.Chat.ChatCompletionOptions.MaxOutputTokenCount">
2988.             <summary>
2989.             An upper bound for the number of tokens that can be generated for a completion, including visible output tokens and, on applicable models, reasoning tokens.
2990.             </summary>
2991.         </member>
2992.         <member name="P:OpenAI.Chat.ChatCompletionOptions.Metadata">
2993.             <summary>
2994.                 Developer-defined tags and values used for filtering completions in the
2995.                 <see href="https://platform.openai.com/chat-completions">OpenAI Platform dashboard</see>.
2996.             </summary>
2997.         </member>
2998.         <member name="P:OpenAI.Chat.ChatCompletionOptions.StoredOutputEnabled">
2999.             <summary>
3000.                 Indicates whether to store the output of this chat completion request for use in
3001.                 <see href="https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/distillation">model distillation</see>
3002.                 or <see href="https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/evals">evals</see>.
3003.             </summary>
3004.         </member>
3005.         <member name="P:OpenAI.Chat.ChatCompletionOptions.ReasoningEffortLevel">
3006.             <summary>
3007.             (o1 and newer reasoning models only) Constrains effort on reasoning for reasoning models.
3008.             Currently supported values are <see cref="P:OpenAI.Chat.ChatReasoningEffortLevel.Low"/>, <see cref="P:OpenAI.Chat.ChatReasoningEffortLevel.Medium"/>, and <see cref="P:OpenAI.Chat.ChatReasoningEffortLevel.High"/>.
3009.             Reducing reasoning effort can result in faster responses and fewer tokens used on reasoning in a response.
3010.             </summary>
3011.         </member>
3012.         <member name="P:OpenAI.Chat.ChatCompletionOptions.ResponseModalities">
3013.             <summary>
3014.             Specifies the content types that the model should generate in its responses.
3015.             </summary>
3016.             <remarks>
3017.             Most models can generate text and the default <c>["text"]</c> value, from <c><see cref="F:OpenAI.Chat.ChatResponseModalities.Text"/></c>, requests this.
3018.             Some models like <c>gpt-4o-audio-preview</c> can also generate audio, and this can be requested by combining <c>["text","audio"]</c> via
3019.             the flags <c><see cref="F:OpenAI.Chat.ChatResponseModalities.Text"/> | <see cref="F:OpenAI.Chat.ChatResponseModalities.Audio"/></c>.
3020.             </remarks>
3021.         </member>
3022.         <member name="T:OpenAI.Chat.ChatFinishReason">
3023.             <summary>
3024.             The reason the model stopped generating tokens. This will be:
3025.             <list type="table">
3026.             <listheader>
3027.                 <member>Property</member>
3028.                 <rest>REST</rest>
3029.                 <cond>Condition</cond>
3030.             </listheader>
3031.             <item>
3032.                 <member><see cref="F:OpenAI.Chat.ChatFinishReason.Stop"/></member>
3033.                 <rest><c>stop</c></rest>
3034.                 <cond>The model encountered a natural stop point or provided stop sequence.</cond>
3035.             </item>
3036.             <item>
3037.                 <member><see cref="F:OpenAI.Chat.ChatFinishReason.Length"/></member>
3038.                 <rest><c>length</c></rest>
3039.                 <cond>The maximum number of tokens specified in the request was reached.</cond>
3040.             </item>
3041.             <item>
3042.                 <member><see cref="F:OpenAI.Chat.ChatFinishReason.ContentFilter"/></member>
3043.                 <rest><c>content_filter</c></rest>
3044.                 <cond>Content was omitted due to a triggered content filter rule.</cond>
3045.             </item>
3046.             <item>
3047.                 <member><see cref="F:OpenAI.Chat.ChatFinishReason.ToolCalls"/></member>
3048.                 <rest><c>tool_calls</c></rest>
3049.                 <cond>
3050.                     With no explicit <c>tool_choice</c>, the model called one or more tools that were defined in the request.
3051.                 </cond>
3052.             </item>
3053.             <item>
3054.                 <member><see cref="F:OpenAI.Chat.ChatFinishReason.FunctionCall"/></member>
3055.                 <rest><c>function_call</c></rest>
3056.                 <cond>(Deprecated) The model called a function that was defined in the request.</cond>
3057.             </item>
3058.             </list>
3059.             </summary>
3060.         </member>
3061.         <member name="F:OpenAI.Chat.ChatFinishReason.Stop">
3062.             <summary>
3063.             Indicates that the model encountered a natural stop point or provided stop sequence.
3064.             </summary>
3065.         </member>
3066.         <member name="F:OpenAI.Chat.ChatFinishReason.Length">
3067.             <summary>
3068.             Indicates that the model reached the maximum number of tokens allowed for the request.
3069.             </summary>
3070.         </member>
3071.         <member name="F:OpenAI.Chat.ChatFinishReason.ContentFilter">
3072.             <summary>
3073.             Indicates that content was omitted due to a triggered content filter rule.
3074.             </summary>
3075.         </member>
3076.         <member name="F:OpenAI.Chat.ChatFinishReason.ToolCalls">
3077.             <summary>
3078.             Indicates that the model called a function that was defined in the request.
3079.             </summary>
3080.             <remarks>
3081.             To resolve tool calls, append the message associated with the tool calls followed by matching instances of
3082.             <see cref="T:OpenAI.Chat.ToolChatMessage"/> for each tool call, then perform another chat completion with the combined
3083.             set of messages.
3084.             <para>
3085.             <b>Note</b>: <see cref="F:OpenAI.Chat.ChatFinishReason.ToolCalls"/> is <i>not</i> provided as the <c>finish_reason</c> if the model calls a
3086.             tool in response to an explicit <c>tool_choice</c> via <see cref="P:OpenAI.Chat.ChatCompletionOptions.ToolChoice"/>.
3087.             In that case, calling the specified tool is assumed and the expected reason is <see cref="F:OpenAI.Chat.ChatFinishReason.Stop"/>.
3088.             </para>
3089.             </remarks>
3090.         </member>
3091.         <member name="F:OpenAI.Chat.ChatFinishReason.FunctionCall">
3092.             <summary>
3093.             Indicates that the model called a function that was defined in the request.
3094.             </summary>
3095.             <remarks>
3096.             To resolve a function call, append the message associated with the function call followed by a
3097.             <see cref="T:OpenAI.Chat.FunctionChatMessage"/> with the appropriate name and arguments, then perform another chat
3098.             completion with the combined set of messages.
3099.             </remarks>
3100.         </member>

3121.         <member name="T:OpenAI.Chat.ChatFunctionCall">
3122.             <summary>
3123.             Represents an <c>assistant</c> call against a supplied <see cref="T:OpenAI.Chat.ChatFunction"/> that is needed by the
3124.             model to continue the logical conversation.
3125.             </summary>
3126.             <remarks>
3127.             <para>
3128.                 Note that <c>functions</c> are deprecated in favor of <c>tools</c> and using <see cref="T:OpenAI.Chat.ChatTool"/>
3129.                 instances with <see cref="P:OpenAI.Chat.ChatCompletionOptions.Tools"/> will enable the use of <c>tool_calls</c> via
3130.                 <see cref="P:OpenAI.Chat.ChatCompletion.ToolCalls"/> instead of this type.
3131.             </para>
3132.             <para>
3133.                 The model makes a <c>function_call</c> in response to evaluation of supplied <c>name></c> and
3134.                 <c>description</c> information in <c>functions</c> and is resolved by providing a new
3135.                 <see cref="T:OpenAI.Chat.FunctionChatMessage"/> with matching functioning output on a subsequent chat completion
3136.                 request.
3137.             </para>
3138.             </remarks>
3139.         </member>

3148.             function.

3350.         <member name="T:OpenAI.Chat.ChatMessageRole">
3351.             <summary>
3352.             Represents the <c>role</c> of a chat completion message.
3353.             </summary>
3354.             <remarks>
3355.             <list type="table">
3356.             <listheader>
3357.                 <type>Type -</type>
3358.                 <role>Role -</role>
3359.                 <note>Description</note>
3360.             </listheader>
3361.             <item>
3362.                 <type><see cref="F:OpenAI.Chat.ChatMessageRole.Developer"/> -</type>
3363.                 <role><c>developer</c> -</role>
3364.                 <note>Instructions to the model that guide the behavior of future <c>assistant</c> messages. Replaces <c>system</c> in o1 and newer models.</note>
3365.             </item>
3366.             <item>
3367.                 <type><see cref="F:OpenAI.Chat.ChatMessageRole.System"/> -</type>
3368.                 <role><c>system</c> -</role>
3369.                 <note>Instructions to the model that guide the behavior of future <c>assistant</c> messages.</note>
3370.             </item>
3371.             <item>
3372.                 <type><see cref="F:OpenAI.Chat.ChatMessageRole.User"/> -</type>
3373.                 <role><c>user</c> -</role>
3374.                 <note>Input messages from the caller, typically paired with <c>assistant</c> messages in a conversation.</note>
3375.             </item>
3376.             <item>
3377.                 <type><see cref="F:OpenAI.Chat.ChatMessageRole.Assistant"/> -</type>
3378.                 <role><c>assistant</c> -</role>
3379.                 <note>
3380.                     Output messages from the model with responses to the <c>user</c> or calls to tools or functions that are
3381.                     needed to continue the logical conversation.
3382.                 </note>
3383.             </item>
3384.             <item>
3385.                 <type><see cref="F:OpenAI.Chat.ChatMessageRole.Tool"/> -</type>
3386.                 <role><c>tool</c> -</role>
3387.                 <note>
3388.                     Resolution information for a <see cref="T:OpenAI.Chat.ChatToolCall"/> in an earlier
3389.                     <see cref="T:OpenAI.Chat.AssistantChatMessage"/> that was made against a supplied
3390.                     <see cref="T:OpenAI.Chat.ChatTool"/>.
3391.                 </note>
3392.             </item>
3393.             <item>
3394.                 <type><see cref="F:OpenAI.Chat.ChatMessageRole.Function"/> -</type>
3395.                 <role><c>function</c> -</role>
3396.                 <note>
3397.                     Resolution information for a <see cref="T:OpenAI.Chat.ChatFunctionCall"/> in an earlier
3398.                     <see cref="T:OpenAI.Chat.AssistantChatMessage"/> that was made against a supplied
3399.                     <see cref="T:OpenAI.Chat.ChatFunction"/>. Note that <c>functions</c> are deprecated in favor of
3400.                     <c>tool_calls</c>.
3401.                 </note>
3402.             </item>
3403.             </list>
3404.             </remarks>
3405.         </member>

3407.             <summary>

3470.             responses, respectively. The <see cref="M:OpenAI.Chat.AssistantChatMessage.#ctor(OpenAI.Chat.ChatCompletion)"/> constructor overload can also be
3471.             used to automatically populate the appropriate properties from a <see cref="T:OpenAI.Chat.ChatCompletion"/> instance.
3472.             </remarks>

3489.         <member name="T:OpenAI.Chat.ChatResponseFormat">
3490.             <summary>
3491.                 The format that the model should output.
3492.                 <list>

3495.                         text.
3496.                     </item>

3498.                         Call <see cref="M:OpenAI.Chat.ChatResponseFormat.CreateJsonObjectFormat"/> to create a <see cref="T:OpenAI.Chat.ChatResponseFormat"/> requesting
3499.                         valid JSON, a.k.a. JSON mode.
3500.                     </item>
3501.                     <item>
3502.                         Call <see cref="M:OpenAI.Chat.ChatResponseFormat.CreateJsonSchemaFormat(System.String,System.BinaryData,System.String,System.Nullable{System.Boolean})"/> to create a
3503.                         <see cref="T:OpenAI.Chat.ChatResponseFormat"/> requesting adherence to the specified JSON schema,
3504.                         a.k.a. structured outputs.
3505.                     </item>
3506.                 </list>
3507.             </summary>
3508.         </member>
3509.         <member name="M:OpenAI.Chat.ChatResponseFormat.CreateTextFormat">
3510.             <summary> Creates a new <see cref="T:OpenAI.Chat.ChatResponseFormat"/> requesting plain text. </summary>
3511.         </member>
3512.         <member name="M:OpenAI.Chat.ChatResponseFormat.CreateJsonObjectFormat">
3513.             <summary> Creates a new <see cref="T:OpenAI.Chat.ChatResponseFormat"/> requesting valid JSON, a.k.a. JSON mode. </summary>
3514.         </member>
3515.         <member name="M:OpenAI.Chat.ChatResponseFormat.CreateJsonSchemaFormat(System.String,System.BinaryData,System.String,System.Nullable{System.Boolean})">
3516.             <summary>
3517.                 Creates a new <see cref="T:OpenAI.Chat.ChatResponseFormat"/> requesting adherence to the specified JSON schema,
3518.                 a.k.a. structured outputs.
3519.             </summary>
3520.             <param name="jsonSchemaFormatName"> The name of the response format. </param>
3521.             <param name="jsonSchema">
3522.                 <para>
3523.                     The schema of the response format, described as a JSON schema. Learn more in the
3524.                     <see href="https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/structured-outputs">structured outputs guide</see>.
3525.                     and the
3526.                     <see href="https://json-schema.org/understanding-json-schema">JSON schema reference documentation</see>.
3527.                 </para>
3528.                 <para>
3529.                     You can easily create a JSON schema via the factory methods of the <see cref="T:System.BinaryData"/> class, such
3530.                     as <see cref="M:System.BinaryData.FromBytes(System.Byte[])"/> or <see cref="M:System.BinaryData.FromString(System.String)"/>. For
3531.                     example, the following code defines a simple schema for step-by-step responses to math problems:
3532.                     <code>
3533.                         BinaryData jsonSchema = BinaryData.FromBytes("""
3534.                             {
3535.                                 "type": "object",
3536.                                 "properties": {
3537.                                     "steps": {
3538.                                         "type": "array",
3539.                                         "items": {
3540.                                             "type": "object",
3541.                                             "properties": {
3542.                                                 "explanation": {"type": "string"},
3543.                                                 "output": {"type": "string"}
3544.                                             },
3545.                                             "required": ["explanation", "output"],
3546.                                             "additionalProperties": false
3547.                                         }
3548.                                     },
3549.                                     "final_answer": {"type": "string"}
3550.                                 },
3551.                                 "required": ["steps", "final_answer"],
3552.                                 "additionalProperties": false
3553.                             }
3554.                             """U8.ToArray());
3555.                     </code>
3556.                 </para>
3557.             </param>
3558.             <param name="jsonSchemaFormatDescription">
3559.                 The description of what the response format is for, which is used by the model to determine how to respond
3560.                 in the format.
3561.             </param>
3562.             <param name="jsonSchemaIsStrict">
3563.                 <para>
3564.                     Whether to enable strict schema adherence when generating the response. If set to <c>true</c>, the
3565.                     model will follow the exact schema defined in <paramref name="jsonSchema"/>.
3566.                 </para>
3567.                 <para>
3568.                     Only a subset of the JSON schema specification is supported when this is set to <c>true</c>. Learn more
3569.                     in the
3570.                     <see href="https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/structured-outputs">structured outputs guide</see>.
3571.                 </para>
3572.             </param>
3573.             <exception cref="T:System.ArgumentNullException"> <paramref name="jsonSchemaFormatName"/> or <paramref name="jsonSchema"/> is null. </exception>
3574.             <exception cref="T:System.ArgumentException"> <paramref name="jsonSchemaFormatName"/> is an empty string, and was expected to be non-empty. </exception>
3575.         </member>

3728.         <member name="M:OpenAI.Chat.ChatTool.CreateFunctionTool(System.String,System.String,System.BinaryData,System.Nullable{System.Boolean})">
3729.             <summary> Creates a new <see cref="T:OpenAI.Chat.ChatTool"/> representing a function that the model may call. </summary>
3730.             <param name="functionName"> The name of the function. </param>
3731.             <param name="functionDescription">
3732.                 The description of what the function does, which is used by the model to choose when and how to call the
3733.                 function.
3734.             </param>
3735.             <param name="functionParameters">
3736.                 <para>
3737.                     The parameters that the function accepts, which are described as a JSON schema. If omitted, this
3738.                     defines a function with an empty parameter list. Learn more in the
3739.                     <see href="https://platform.openai.com/docs/api-reference/chat/docs/guides/function-calling">function calling guide</see>
3740.                     and the
3741.                     <see href="https://json-schema.org/understanding-json-schema">JSON schema reference documentation</see>.
3742.                 </para>
3743.                 <para>
3744.                     You can easily create a JSON schema via the factory methods of the <see cref="T:System.BinaryData"/> class, such
3745.                     as <see cref="M:System.BinaryData.FromBytes(System.Byte[])"/> or <see cref="M:System.BinaryData.FromString(System.String)"/>. For
3746.                     example, the following code defines a simple schema for a function that takes a customer's order ID as
3747.                     a <c>string</c> parameter:
3748.                     <code>
3749.                         BinaryData functionParameters = BinaryData.FromBytes("""
3750.                             {
3751.                                 "type": "object",
3752.                                 "properties": {
3753.                                     "order_id": {
3754.                                         "type": "string",
3755.                                         "description": "The customer's order ID."
3756.                                     }
3757.                                 },
3758.                                 "required": ["order_id"],
3759.                                 "additionalProperties": false
3760.                             }
3761.                             """u8.ToArray());
3762.                     </code>
3763.                 </para>
3764.             </param>
3765.             <param name="functionSchemaIsStrict">
3766.                 <para>
3767.                     Whether to enable strict schema adherence when generating the function call. If set to <c>true</c>, the
3768.                     model will follow the exact schema defined in <paramref name="functionParameters"/>.
3769.                 </para>
3770.                 <para>
3771.                     Only a subset of the JSON schema specification is supported when this is set to <c>true</c>. Learn more
3772.                     about structured outputs in the
3773.                     <see href="https://platform.openai.com/docs/api-reference/chat/docs/guides/function-calling">function calling guide</see>.
3774.                 </para>
3775.             </param>
3776.         </member>

3808.                 The arguments that model is calling the function with, which are generated by the model in JSON format.

3815.         <member name="T:OpenAI.Chat.ChatToolChoice">

3895.         <member name="M:OpenAI.Chat.AssistantChatMessage.#ctor(System.Collections.Generic.IEnumerable{OpenAI.Chat.ChatMessageContentPart})">

3963.         <member name="T:OpenAI.Chat.ChatMessage">
3964.             <summary>
3965.             A common, base representation of a message provided as input into a chat completion request.
3966.             </summary>
3967.             <remarks>
3968.             <list type="table">
3969.             <listheader>
3970.                 <type>Type -</type>
3971.                 <role>Role -</role>
3972.                 <note>Description</note>
3973.             </listheader>
3974.             <item>
3975.                 <type><see cref="T:OpenAI.Chat.SystemChatMessage"/> -</type>
3976.                 <role><c>system</c> -</role>
3977.                 <note>Instructions to the model that guide the behavior of future <c>assistant</c> messages.</note>
3978.             </item>
3979.             <item>
3980.                 <type><see cref="T:OpenAI.Chat.UserChatMessage"/> -</type>
3981.                 <role><c>user</c> -</role>
3982.                 <note>Input messages from the caller, typically paired with <c>assistant</c> messages in a conversation.</note>
3983.             </item>
3984.             <item>
3985.                 <type><see cref="T:OpenAI.Chat.AssistantChatMessage"/> -</type>
3986.                 <role><c>assistant</c> -</role>
3987.                 <note>
3988.                     Output messages from the model with responses to the <c>user</c> or calls to tools or functions that are
3989.                     needed to continue the logical conversation.
3990.                 </note>
3991.             </item>
3992.             <item>
3993.                 <type><see cref="T:OpenAI.Chat.ToolChatMessage"/> -</type>
3994.                 <role><c>tool</c> -</role>
3995.                 <note>
3996.                     Resolution information for a <see cref="T:OpenAI.Chat.ChatToolCall"/> in an earlier
3997.                     <see cref="T:OpenAI.Chat.AssistantChatMessage"/> that was made against a supplied
3998.                     <see cref="T:OpenAI.Chat.ChatTool"/>.
3999.                 </note>
4000.             </item>
4001.             <item>
4002.                 <type><see cref="T:OpenAI.Chat.FunctionChatMessage"/> -</type>
4003.                 <role><c>function</c> -</role>
4004.                 <note>
4005.                     Resolution information for a <see cref="T:OpenAI.Chat.ChatFunctionCall"/> in an earlier
4006.                     <see cref="T:OpenAI.Chat.AssistantChatMessage"/> that was made against a supplied
4007.                     <see cref="T:OpenAI.Chat.ChatFunction"/>. Note that <c>functions</c> are deprecated in favor of
4008.                     <c>tool_calls</c>.
4009.                 </note>
4010.             </item>
4011.             </list>
4012.             </remarks>
4013.         </member>

4182.         <member name="T:OpenAI.Chat.ToolChatMessage">
4183.             <summary>
4184.             Represents a chat message of the <c>tool</c> role as supplied to a chat completion request. A tool message
4185.             encapsulates a resolution of a <see cref="T:OpenAI.Chat.ChatToolCall"/> made by the model. The typical interaction flow featuring
4186.             tool messages is:
4187.             <list type="number">
4188.             <item> A <see cref="T:OpenAI.Chat.ToolChatMessage"/> provides a <see cref="T:OpenAI.Chat.ToolChatMessage"/> on a request; </item>
4189.             <item>
4190.                 Based on the <c>name</c> and <c>description</c> information of provided tools, the model responds with one or
4191.                 more <see cref="T:OpenAI.Chat.ChatToolCall"/> instances that need to be resolved to continue the logical conversation;
4192.             </item>
4193.             <item>
4194.                 For each <see cref="T:OpenAI.Chat.ChatToolCall"/>, the matching tool is invoked and its output is supplied back to the model
4195.                 via a <see cref="T:OpenAI.Chat.ToolChatMessage"/> to resolve the tool call and allow the logical conversation to
4196.                 continue.
4197.             </item>
4198.             </list>
4199.             </summary>
4200.         </member>

4355.         <member name="P:OpenAI.Chat.InternalCreateChatCompletionStreamResponseChoice.FinishReason">
4356.             <summary>
4357.             The reason the model stopped generating tokens. This will be `stop` if the model hit a natural stop point or a provided stop sequence,
4358.             `length` if the maximum number of tokens specified in the request was reached,
4359.             `content_filter` if content was omitted due to a flag from our content filters,
4360.             `tool_calls` if the model called a tool, or `function_call` (deprecated) if the model called a function.
4361.             </summary>
4362.         </member>
4363.         <member name="T:OpenAI.Chat.StreamingChatCompletionUpdate">
4364.             <summary> An incremental update corresponding to a streaming chat completion generated by the model. </summary>
4365.         </member>
4366.         <member name="P:OpenAI.Chat.StreamingChatCompletionUpdate.Object">
4367.             <summary> The object type, which is always `chat.completion.chunk`. </summary>
4368.         </member>
4369.         <member name="P:OpenAI.Chat.StreamingChatCompletionUpdate.CreatedAt">
4370.             <summary> The timestamp when the chat completion was created. </summary>
4371.             <remarks> Each update has the same timestamp. </remarks>
4372.         </member>
4373.         <member name="P:OpenAI.Chat.StreamingChatCompletionUpdate.Usage">
4374.             <summary> The token usage statistics for the entire chat completion. </summary>
4375.             <remarks> Present only in the last streaming update. </remarks>
4376.         </member>
4377.         <member name="P:OpenAI.Chat.StreamingChatCompletionUpdate.FinishReason">
4378.             <summary>
4379.                 The reason the model stopped generating tokens. This will be:
4380.                 <list>
4381.                     <item>
4382.                         <see cref="F:OpenAI.Chat.ChatFinishReason.Stop"/> if the model hit a natural stop point or a provided stop sequence
4383.                     </item>
4384.                     <item>
4385.                         <see cref="F:OpenAI.Chat.ChatFinishReason.Length"/> if the maximum number of tokens specified in the request was reached
4386.                     </item>
4387.                     <item>
4388.                         <see cref="F:OpenAI.Chat.ChatFinishReason.ContentFilter"/> if content was omitted due to a flag from our content filters
4389.                     </item>
4390.                     <item>
4391.                         <see cref="F:OpenAI.Chat.ChatFinishReason.ToolCalls"/> if the model called a tool
4392.                     </item>
4393.                     <item>
4394.                         <see cref="F:OpenAI.Chat.ChatFinishReason.FunctionCall"/> (obsolte) if the model called a function
4395.                     </item>
4396.                 </list>
4397.             </summary>
4398.         </member>
4399.         <member name="P:OpenAI.Chat.StreamingChatCompletionUpdate.ContentTokenLogProbabilities">
4400.             <summary> The message content tokens with log probability information. </summary>
4401.         </member>
4402.         <member name="P:OpenAI.Chat.StreamingChatCompletionUpdate.RefusalTokenLogProbabilities">
4403.             <summary> The message refusal tokens with log probability information. </summary>
4404.         </member>
4405.         <member name="P:OpenAI.Chat.StreamingChatCompletionUpdate.Role">
4406.             <summary> The role of the author of this message. </summary>
4407.             <remarks> Typically present in a single streaming update but applicable to the entire chat completion. </remarks>
4408.         </member>
4409.         <member name="P:OpenAI.Chat.StreamingChatCompletionUpdate.ContentUpdate">
4410.             <summary> The contents of the message update. </summary>
4411.             <remarks>
4412.                 Each streaming update contains only a small portion of tokens. To reconstitute the entire chat completion,
4413.                 all <see cref="P:OpenAI.Chat.StreamingChatCompletionUpdate.ContentUpdate"/> values across streaming updates must be combined.
4414.             </remarks>
4415.         </member>
4416.         <member name="P:OpenAI.Chat.StreamingChatCompletionUpdate.ToolCallUpdates">
4417.             <summary> The tool calls generated by the model, such as function calls. </summary>
4418.         </member>
4419.         <member name="P:OpenAI.Chat.StreamingChatCompletionUpdate.RefusalUpdate">
4420.             <summary> The refusal message generated by the model. </summary>
4421.         </member>
4422.         <member name="P:OpenAI.Chat.StreamingChatCompletionUpdate.OutputAudioUpdate">
4423.             <summary>
4424.             Incremental output audio generated by the model. Only expected when output audio has been requested via providing
4425.             <see cref="T:OpenAI.Chat.ChatAudioOptions"/> to <see cref="P:OpenAI.Chat.ChatCompletionOptions.AudioOptions"/> and only available with
4426.             supported models.
4427.             </summary>
4428.         </member>
4429.         <member name="P:OpenAI.Chat.StreamingChatFunctionCallUpdate.FunctionName">
4430.             <summary> The name of the function to call. </summary>
4431.         </member>
4432.         <member name="P:OpenAI.Chat.StreamingChatFunctionCallUpdate.FunctionArgumentsUpdate">
4433.             <summary>
4434.             Gets a function arguments fragment associated with this update.
4435.             </summary>
4436.             <remarks>
4437.                 <para>
4438.                     Each update contains only a small number of tokens. When presenting or reconstituting a full, streamed
4439.                     arguments body, all <see cref="P:OpenAI.Chat.StreamingChatFunctionCallUpdate.FunctionArgumentsUpdate"/> values should be combined.
4440.                 </para>
4441.                 <para>
4442.                     As is the case for non-streaming <see cref="P:OpenAI.Chat.ChatFunctionCall.FunctionArguments"/>, the content provided
4443.                     for function arguments is not guaranteed to be well-formed JSON or to contain expected data. Callers
4444.                     should validate function arguments before using them.
4445.                 </para>
4446.             </remarks>
4447.         </member>
4448.         <member name="T:OpenAI.Chat.StreamingChatOutputAudioUpdate">
4449.             <summary>
4450.             Represents an audio update in a streaming chat response.
4451.             </summary>
4452.         </member>
4453.         <member name="P:OpenAI.Chat.StreamingChatOutputAudioUpdate.TranscriptUpdate">
4454.             <summary>
4455.             The next, incremental audio transcript part from the streaming response. <see cref="P:OpenAI.Chat.StreamingChatOutputAudioUpdate.TranscriptUpdate"/> payloads
4456.             across all received <see cref="T:OpenAI.Chat.StreamingChatCompletionUpdate"/> instances should be concatenated to form the
4457.             full response audio transcript.
4458.             </summary>
4459.         </member>
4460.         <member name="P:OpenAI.Chat.StreamingChatOutputAudioUpdate.AudioBytesUpdate">
4461.             <summary>
4462.             The next, incremental response audio data chunk from the streaming response. <see cref="P:OpenAI.Chat.StreamingChatOutputAudioUpdate.AudioBytesUpdate"/> payloads
4463.             across all received <see cref="T:OpenAI.Chat.StreamingChatCompletionUpdate"/> instances should be concatenated to form the
4464.             full response audio.
4465.             </summary>
4466.         </member>
4467.         <member name="T:OpenAI.Chat.StreamingChatToolCallUpdate">
4468.             <summary> A streaming update representing part of a tool call made by the model. </summary>
4469.         </member>
4470.         <member name="P:OpenAI.Chat.StreamingChatToolCallUpdate.Kind">
4471.             <summary> The kind of tool.Currently, only<see cref="F:OpenAI.Chat.ChatToolCallKind.Function"/> is supported. </summary>
4472.         </member>
4473.         <member name="P:OpenAI.Chat.StreamingChatToolCallUpdate.ToolCallId">
4474.             <summary>
4475.             The unique identifier of the tool call being streamed, as used with e.g.
4476.             <see cref="M:OpenAI.Chat.ChatToolCall.CreateFunctionToolCall(System.String,System.String,System.BinaryData)"/> or
4477.             <see cref="M:OpenAI.Chat.ToolChatMessage.#ctor(System.String,System.String)"/>.
4478.             </summary>
4479.         </member>
4480.         <member name="P:OpenAI.Chat.StreamingChatToolCallUpdate.FunctionName">
4481.             <summary>
4482.             The name of the tool that the streaming tool call requests invocation of.
4483.             </summary>
4484.         </member>
4485.         <member name="P:OpenAI.Chat.StreamingChatToolCallUpdate.FunctionArgumentsUpdate">
4486.             <summary>
4487.                 The update to the arguments that the model is calling the function with, which are generated by the model
4488.                 in JSON format. As part of a streaming response, the arguments are chunked and streamed across streaming
4489.                 updates, and must therefore be accumulated in order to reconstruct them. Note that the model does not
4490.                 always generate valid JSON, and may hallucinate parameters not defined by your function schema. Validate
4491.                 the arguments in your code before calling your function.
4492.             </summary>
4493.         </member>

6804.             In contrast to other clients, <see cref="T:OpenAI.Realtime.RealtimeClient"/> does not manage input/output operations directly,
6805.             instead delegating stateful interaction to a child session object.
6806.             </para>
6807.             Create <see cref="T:System.IDisposable"/> instances of <see cref="T:OpenAI.Realtime.RealtimeSessionClient"/> using methods like
6808.             <see cref="!:RealtimeClient.StartConversationSessionAsync(string, System.Threading.CancellationToken)"/> and
6809.             <see cref="!:RealtimeClient.StartTranscriptionSessionAsync(System.Threading.CancellationToken)"/>

6853.             The value to use for the <c>OpenAI-Organization</c> request header. Users who belong to multiple organizations
6854.             can set this value to specify which organization is used for an API request. Usage from these API requests will
6855.             count against the specified organization's quota. If not set, the header will be omitted, and the default
6856.             organization will be billed. You can change your default organization in your user settings.
6857.             <see href="https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/production-best-practices/setting-up-your-organization">Learn more</see>.
6858.             </summary>
6859.         </member>
6860.         <member name="P:OpenAI.OpenAIClientOptions.ProjectId">
6861.             <summary>
6862.             The value to use for the <c>OpenAI-Project</c> request header. Users who are accessing their projects through
6863.             their legacy user API key can set this value to specify which project is used for an API request. Usage from
6864.             these API requests will count as usage for the specified project. If not set, the header will be omitted, and
6865.             the default project will be accessed.
6866.             </summary>
6867.         </member>
6868.         <member name="P:OpenAI.OpenAIClientOptions.UserAgentApplicationId">
6869.             <summary>
6870.             An optional application ID to use as part of the request User-Agent header.
6871.             </summary>
6872.         </member>
